insight: The Q defense algorithm (auto-defend) in Prismata has a known weakness with fragile units: it will absorb damage onto a Tia Threnody instead of an Infusion Grid, which is incorrect since losing Tia costs too much defense tempo compared to losing the Infusion Grid.
author: jamberine
context: Players discussing cases where the Q defense auto-assign makes suboptimal choices with fragile units like Tia Threnody.

insight: The Q defense algorithm will incorrectly absorb damage onto an Odin instead of a Xaetron when forced to choose between the two, whereas back-lining on Xaetron is almost always clearly correct since Odin has typically already been clicked if the player wanted to defend with it.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy responding to Terra Reveene about the Q defense algorithm's decision-making between Odin and Xaetron absorption.

insight: The Q defense algorithm can sacrifice a Steelsplitter to keep a Wall alive, which is a clear misdefense — it should save the Steelsplitter over the Wall in most situations.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy recounting a personal experience where Q defense soaked with a Splitter to leave a Wall alive, which is the wrong priority.

insight: The Q defense algorithm can incorrectly sacrifice Forcefields over Walls when multiple defenders are present — it may lose 3 Forcefields over 2 Walls, which is usually the wrong choice since Forcefields are more valuable defenders.
author: apooche
context: apooche explaining specific failure modes of Q defense to sorawotobu, including losing Forcefields over Walls.

insight: The Q defense algorithm can unnecessarily sacrifice granularity: when a player has a mix of Infusion units and Walls, it may lose an Infusion and 2 Engineers instead of the correct 2 Walls, needlessly giving up granular attack value.
author: apooche
context: apooche detailing Q defense failure modes where it misallocates absorb between Infusions and Walls.

insight: Expert players recommend using Q defense only when you already know the correct defense and are confident Q will match it — you should immediately verify the result. Q defense is only unambiguously correct when there is exactly one valid way to defend.
author: apooche
context: apooche and crash_overlord discussing when Q defense is and is not safe to rely on.

insight: Community members considered Master Bot to be approximately Tier 6 in strength, strong enough that it takes new players many hours (sometimes 40+ hours of play) to beat it consistently, making it a meaningful skill benchmark before transitioning to ranked play.
author: ruinedshadows
context: ruinedshadows and zheliu discussing how long it takes new players to beat Master Bot consistently, with zheliu noting a forum post of a player with 40 hours still unable to do so.

insight: In Prismata, losing Engineers, Walls, or any unit to absorb all constitutes 'taking damage' — a common new player mistake is treating only non-Wall unit losses as damage, leading to unnecessary overdefending with extra Walls.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 observing a new streamer who called losing Walls 'not taking damage' and only losing other units as 'taking damage', explaining why they over-built Walls.

insight: There are no true mirror matches in Prismata: because players have asymmetric starting positions (Player 1 vs Player 2), even games where both players buy the same units involve asymmetric decisions. Losing a 'mirror' is very likely due to incorrect timings rather than the strategy itself being wrong.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 responding to discussion about mirror matches and whether copying opponents is valid strategy.

insight: Supply is a meaningful strategic resource in Prismata — experienced players can deliberately win positions that would be losing in an infinite-supply game by managing supply counts, meaning supply exhaustion is not just an accident but can be a planned win condition.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 explaining that supply constraints are a real strategic axis, not just an incidental rule.
